---
title: "Methods & Sources"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

## Data

A single Esri file geodatabase layer, `CP_Hiring_Exercise_Missoula`
(**32,974 parcels**, NAD83 State Plane Montana, US feet), with an
accompanying field map. Fields used: existing use
(`LandUseBuildingType_All`), dwelling units, assessed land/building/total
value, acres, Growth Policy future land use (`Land_Use`), current zoning
(`Zoning_Mstr`), percent of parcel in floodway (`PercentageFlood`), and
percent in steep slopes > 25% (`PercentSlope`).

## Cleaning decisions

| Quirk | Treatment |
|---|---|
| ~900 features stored as curve geometries (`MultiSurface`) | Linearized to polygons on read (GDAL `forceTo`) |
| `PercentageFlood` / `PercentSlope` populated only where a constraint exists | `NA` → 0% (verified: nulls mean "no mapped constraint") |
| `YearBuilt = 0` sentinel | → `NA` |
| Invalid rings / self-intersections | Repaired with `st_make_valid()` on the invalid subset |
| Empty geometries | Dropped |

## Plan-enabled capacity

For each parcel whose Growth Policy designation calls for housing:

> **capacity** = developable acres × assumed du/ac − existing units,
> floored at zero

where *developable acres* = acres × (1 − max(flood share, slope share)).
Taking the max of the two constraint shares is conservative without
double-counting (their overlap is unknown).

**Allowable densities** come from the adopted **Our Missoula 2035 Growth
Policy** designation ranges, as those ranges are applied in the City's
own annexation and rezoning staff reports (e.g., "Residential Medium
Density (3 to 11 dwelling units per acre)... Residential High Density
(24 to 43 dwelling units per acre) (Our Missoula 2035)"). Capacity uses
the **midpoint** of each range — a deliberately conservative "expected"
density rather than the plan's ceiling:

| Designation | Policy range (du/ac) | Used |
|---|---|---|
| Residential Low | 1–2 | 1.5 |
| Residential Medium | 3–11 | 7 |
| Residential Medium-High | 12–23 | 17.5 |
| Residential High | 24–43 | 33.5 |
| Neighborhood Mixed Use | carries medium-high (12–23) | 17.5 |
| Community Mixed Use | carries high (24–43 in aligned zoning) | 33.5 |
| Urban Center | not stated in the Policy | 43 |

Urban Center has no du/ac in the Policy; it is pinned at 43 — the
highest density the Policy quotes anywhere — which is conservative for
the downtown designation. Values are centralized in `code/00_setup.R`.
All other designations (including the Sxwtpqyen Area's own designation,
industrial, parks, open/resource) contribute zero capacity — a
conservative choice that likely *understates* the total.

## The opportunity screen

A parcel counts as an **opportunity** when all of the following hold:

1. **Housing-eligible** future land-use designation (table above);
2. **Economically soft** — vacant, or improvement-to-land ratio (ILR:
   assessed building value ÷ assessed land value) **< 1.0**, i.e., the
   dirt is worth more than what stands on it;
3. **Not excluded** — public/institutional land, parks, open space and
   recreation, master condo records (which would double-count their child
   units), and **mobile homes** (naturally occurring affordable housing —
   flagged separately as displacement risk, never as opportunity);
4. **Not constrained** — no more than 50% of the parcel in floodway or
   steep slope; capacity on the remainder is already discounted;
5. **Material** — capacity gap of at least one unit.

Opportunity parcels are typed **vacant vs. underbuilt** and sized
**infill (≤ 5 ac) vs. large site (> 5 ac)**. The 5-acre line separates
parcels a single owner or small builder can develop under by-right rules
from sites that realistically need master planning, subdivision, and new
internal infrastructure.

## Hot-spot analysis (Getis-Ord Gi\*)

Plan-enabled units on opportunity parcels are summed to a **1,000-ft
hexagonal grid** (~20-acre cells; 1,188 hexes contain at least one
housing-eligible parcel). Gi\* runs on the hex values with queen
contiguity, self-inclusive weights; classes are reported at 90/95/99%
confidence.

**Why hexes rather than parcels?** Parcel-level unit gap is extremely
right-skewed (median 1, maximum ~3,200), and parcels vary enormously in
size, so a parcel-level statistic mostly re-identifies individual
mega-parcels and their immediate neighbors. Aggregating first asks the
policy-relevant question — *which places, at the scale of a block cluster,
concentrate capacity* — and yields legible corridors instead of scattered
outliers. A uniform grid also equalizes the spatial support underlying each
observation (parcels differ in area by four orders of magnitude), which
contiguity-based weights otherwise distort.

**Why Gi\* rather than a LISA map?** The question this analysis asks is
specific: *where do high-capacity places sit next to other high-capacity
places* — LISA's **high-high** quadrant. A Gi\* hot spot is precisely
that: because the statistic sums each hex *together with its neighbors*,
a significant positive z-score can only occur where high values cluster
against other high values. So Gi\* delivers the high-high answer
directly, with a single confidence-graded class that maps cleanly.

LISA (local Moran's I) reports the same high-high clusters but adds
three more classes — low-low, and the high-low / low-high spatial
*outliers*. Those are valuable diagnostics (an isolated mega-parcel in a
low-capacity neighborhood is a high-low outlier), but for a policy
audience they quadruple the legend to answer a question nobody asked
here. The one thing LISA would add — cold spots (low-low) — turns out
empty: no low-capacity cluster reaches significance in this data, which
is why the map legend shows only capacity clusters. Running LISA as a
robustness check on the hex grid is a natural QC step for the regional
expansion.

## Reproducibility

```sh
Rscript code/run_all.R   # raw .gdb -> derived data -> figures -> map
```

Numbered scripts with all assumptions centralized in `code/00_setup.R`;
the exact package environment is recorded in `output/session_info.txt`.
The website is rendered with Quarto from the committed figure and map
outputs, so rendering never re-runs the analysis.

## Limitations

- **Plan-implied, not predicted.** Capacity measures what the adopted
  future land-use map makes room for — not ownership, feasibility, or
  infrastructure timing.
- **Densities are range midpoints**, not the plan's ceiling — using the
  top of each designation range would roughly double the headline
  capacity. Urban Center's value is an assumption (the Policy states no
  du/ac for it).
- **Assessed values** proxy economic softness; they lag the market and
  compress at the top.
- **The Sxwtpqyen Area** (766 parcels) is conservatively excluded from
  capacity; the Mullan-area master plan is largely residential, so totals
  are understated.
- **Zoning is not analyzed here** — the story is deliberately framed
  against the Growth Policy. A zoning-vs-plan entitlement-gap analysis is
  the natural next phase.
